It's quarter past seven on a Tuesday and I'm in my classroom in Utrecht before anyone arrives, the radiator ticking, a mug of tea going cold while I move the name cards around for group work. So a normal day: kids in by 8:30, and the first hour is the part I'd never trade — reading circle, eight-year-olds arguing about whether a character was actually being kind. The thing that's different from what you're picturing now is how much of the flat admin is just gone. The planning tool drafts my lesson skeletons overnight and flags which three kids stalled on fractions yesterday, so I walk in already knowing where to kneel down. What it can't do is notice that Sem went quiet because his parents are splitting up. That's become the whole job, honestly — the noticing. What's pulling you toward the classroom specifically?

Honest answer, and not the one I'd have given you ten years ago: spend less energy on the polished lesson plans and more on the messy human stuff — reading a room, sitting with a kid who's melting down without flinching, running a conversation where you're not in control of where it goes. The tools handle differentiation and worksheets now; in my training year back in 2030 we were already letting the system generate the materials and the assessment got reweighted around how well you actually facilitate. So: drama, anything that puts you in front of restless people, even tutoring one difficult kid for a term teaches you more than another module. The classroom-management theory you'll read is fine, but it lives in your hands, not your notes. Where do you feel least ready right now — the teaching part, or the holding-twenty-nine-of-them part?

How fast it stopped being about delivering content. I used to think being a good teacher meant knowing things and explaining them well — and a decade in, the explaining is the part most easily handed off. A kid can ask the wall a question and get a patient answer at exactly their level, all day. What surprised me was that this didn't make me redundant; it made the human bits louder. Last winter a girl named Noor wouldn't read aloud, ever, and there's no tool for the afternoon she finally did, voice shaking, and the class just… waited for her. That's the work now. The melancholy version: some days I miss feeling like the expert in the room. The truer version is I'm something harder to replace instead. It's one version of where this could go for you, not a forecast — but if you want, I'll tell you about the parts that wear me down too, because they're real.
